sources.title: Much Ado About Nothing by William Shakespeare
sources.rightsBasis: Underlying work is public domain in the UK; Project Gutenberg plain-text edition; only short attributed excerpts are retained.

evidence.locator: Act 1, Scene 1; Don Pedro agrees to woo Hero for Claudio
evidence.anchor: I will assume thy part in some disguise, And tell fair Hero I am Claudio

evidence.locator: Act 2, Scene 3; Benedick overhears the staged conversation
evidence.anchor: I should think this a gull, but that the white-bearded fellow speaks it

evidence.locator: Act 3, Scene 1; Beatrice decides to return Benedick’s love
evidence.anchor: And, Benedick, love on; I will requite thee

evidence.locator: Act 3, Scene 3; Borachio explains the deception

evidence.anchor: Hero itself can blot out Hero’s virtue.

evidence.locator: Act 3, Scene 3; the Watch hears Borachio confess
evidence.anchor: I have tonight wooed Margaret, the Lady Hero’s gentlewoman, by the name of Hero
I do love nothing in the world so well as you: is not that strange?
evidence.requiredAnswer: I do love nothing in the world so well as you: is not that strange?

evidence.locator: Act 2, Scene 1; Claudio responds to the match
evidence.anchor: Silence is the perfectest herald of joy
evidence.requiredAnswer: Silence is the perfectest herald of joy

evidence.locator: Act 5, Scene 4; Benedick reverses his old position
Act 2, Scene 3; Benedick changes his mind
evidence.anchor: When I said I would die a bachelor, I did not think I should live till I were married.
evidence.requiredAnswer: When I said I would die a bachelor, I did not think I should live till I were married.

evidence.locator: Act 5, Scene 3; Claudio reads the epitaph
evidence.anchor: Done to death by slanderous tongues
evidence.requiredAnswer: Done to death by slanderous tongues

evidence.anchor: I love you with so much of my heart that none is left to protest.
evidence.requiredAnswer: I love you with so much of my heart that none is left to protest.
